title: Spire’s End - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mkJ-QgvLtyk

Daniel (@dungeondive) turns a fresh eye on **Spire's End**, a card-based adventure game by Gregory Favreau with genuinely arresting black, red and grey artwork by Benjamin Weissman — the sort of art that stopped him in his tracks and sent him hunting down a copy directly from the designer. He frames it honestly as one of those odd "not-really-a-review" videos for a game that arrived through Kickstarter and may be hard to find, but his enthusiasm is real.

Spire's End sits in the growing card-based adventure genre alongside Escape the Dark Castle and Sector, but pitched a notch more complex. You work through six chapters of a large story deck, accompanied by allies who double as your lives, spending health as a resource to fuel combat attacks resolved on a (frequently cursed) D8. He's charmed by clever touches — key dice that let you roll more the more keys you hold, deliberately "misprinted" lived-in cards, and a cohesive design aesthetic where even the rules live on the oversized cards. His main gripe: combat drags a turn or two too long. Overall verdict: strongly positive, with high hopes for the design team.

----

Spire's End keeps everything — even the rulebook — on its oversized cards for the sake of a unified aesthetic. Do you love that kind of total design commitment, or would you rather just have a proper rulebook?
